metadata.title: The Role of Vitamins in Sepsis: A Narrative Review.

text: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) remains a leading cause of death and disability worldwide, resulting in long-term cognitive, motor, sensory, and psychosocial impairments among survivors. In both high- and low-to-middle-income countries, severe TBI (Glasgow Coma Scale = 3–8) is associated with prolonged hospitalization, intensive care requirements, and poor functional recovery, making early optimization of in-hospital management essential [ 1 – 3 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Following severe TBI, patients experience a marked hypermetabolic and hypercatabolic response like that seen after major burns covering 20–40% of body surface area [ 1 , 4 ]. This metabolic crisis leads to elevated energy expenditure, accelerated skeletal muscle breakdown, and difficulty achieving nitrogen balance despite aggressive nutritional support [ 4 ]. Malnutrition occurs in up to 76% of patients with severe TBI and is linked to higher mortality and worse outcomes [ 5 – 9 ]; therefore, early nutritional support plays a vital therapeutic role.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Immunonutrition, a targeted nutritional approach incorporating glutamine, arginine, ribonucleic acids, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (omega-3 PUFAs), has gained attention for its potential to modulate post-TBI inflammation [ 1 , 5 ]. These nutrients may act synergistically to improve immune response and reduce oxidative stress [ 5 , 10 , 11 ]. The secondary injury phase of TBI is characterized by neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and excitotoxicity [ 2 , 3 ], processes that immunonutrition may attenuate by replenishing depleted substrates and supporting antioxidant defenses [ 5 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Clinical studies have shown that immunonutrition can decrease inflammatory markers such as interleukin-6 (IL-6) and increase antioxidant levels like glutathione [ 1 ]. However, most available studies have examined isolated nutrients rather than multi-component formulations, and findings remain inconsistent [ 12 – 14 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Despite increasing interest, the evidence regarding combined immunonutrient interventions in hospitalized severe TBI patients remains limited and fragmented. This gap underscores the need for a comprehensive synthesis of the available data. Therefore, the present systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the effects of immunonutrition on inflammatory markers and clinical outcomes in patients with severe TBI across randomized and observational studies.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and cohort studies (prospective or retrospective) were eligible if they enrolled hospitalized patients diagnosed with severe TBI who received immunonutrition and reported at least one predefined inflammatory biomarker or clinical outcome. Due to the limited number of RCTs in this field, high-quality observational studies were also included to provide broader evidence base while maintaining methodological rigor. The eligible studies had to meet the Participants, Interventions, Comparisons, and Outcomes (PICO) criteria [ 15 ], as outlined below:

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Children or adults with a documented diagnosis of TBI, regardless of injury mechanism or surgical intervention.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Studies in which the intervention group received enteral feeding labeled as immunonutrition after TBI, or standard feeding enriched with arginine, glutamine, or omega-3 PUFAs, either alone or in combination. Even studies that administered each of these nutrients via parenteral routes were considered.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The selected studies should be categorized into three main comparator groups: (1) comparison with standard feeding (2), comparison with placebo, or (3) comparison with a control group that receives no intervention.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Inflammatory/nutritional markers: Interleukins, particularly IL-6, C reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), albumin, serum neurofilament light chain (NFL), and other reported cytokines or oxidative stress parameters, when available.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Clinical outcomes: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), morbidity measures (including nosocomial infections), intensive care unit (ICU) length of stay (LOS), hospital LOS, ICU mortality, in-hospital mortality, and anthropometric indices when available.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Two expert investigators independently and meticulously searched three databases: PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus, using the following keywords: Immunonutrition, TBI, “Traumatic brain injury,” Inflammation, Clinical outcomes, and Surgery. The initial combined term search yielded no results; therefore, related nutrient specific terms (e.g., Omega-3, “Omega 3,” PUFA, EPA, DHA, Arginine, L-arginine, “L arginine,” Glutamine) were searched alongside TBI related terms. The detailed PubMed search history is provided in S2 Appendix. In accordance with Cochrane recommendations for systematic reviews [ 16 ], we imposed no time or other limitations on our search, which was conducted using the text word command. Then, the funded documents were manually refined based on predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Additionally, some documents located in the reference lists of certain retrieved studies were considered and added to the core database, if applicable, in accordance with the norms of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. All retrieved studies underwent review at the title/abstract level by two independent expert investigators. Ultimately, after collaborative discussion, more relevant studies were reviewed at the full-text level. This search continued until January 28, 2025.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The overall framework of the present systematic review, from study identification and screening to study inclusion, adhered to the latest version of the PRISMA 2020 statement [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the present systematic review and meta-analysis was design accordance to the PRISMA 2020 checklist, data in S1 Appendix. After two independent investigators carefully reviewed the retrieved studies, final selections were made based on predefined criteria, and the reasons for the exclusion of certain studies were described. Reviewers engaged in discussions and consultations with each other at the end of each step of the study selection process. Finally, if any conflicts arose, they corresponded with the principal investigators of the studies for clarification.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The two reviewers independently extracted data concerning the study population, sample size, interventions, comparators, and potential biases from various studies. They also gathered the mean standard deviations, odds ratios, and confidence intervals of the outcomes for each selected article, following the predefined data collection framework introduced by The Cochrane Collaboration [ 17 ]. All authors of the present work studied and evaluated the extracted data, engaging in a comprehensive discussion about their findings. Any confusions or disagreements were resolved through consultation with the principal investigator. The extracted data were entered into separate Excel sheets for all outcomes and subsequently sent to an expert analyst for meta-analysis.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Based on the Cochrane guidelines for assessing the risk of bias [ 18 , 19 ], the quality of the included studies in terms of detecting potential risks of bias was evaluated by two independent investigators across the following domains: selection bias, performance bias, detection bias, attrition bias, reporting bias, bias due to confounding, bias in the classification of interventions, deviations from intended interventions, missing data, measurement of outcomes, and bias in the selection of reported results. Accordingly, the studies were assessed as having low, moderate, or high risk of bias. These assessments guided the selection of meta-analytic models: outcomes with low heterogeneity (I² ≤ 50%) and studies with low to moderate risk of bias were analyzed using a fixed-effect model, whereas outcomes exhibiting higher heterogeneity or methodological differences were analyzed using a random-effects model. Furthermore, the certainty of evidence was assessed using the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) approach. According to the GRADE classification, the potential limitations of the body of evidence were evaluated across five categories: limitations in study design, inconsistency, imprecision, indirectness, and publication bias. For each of these categories, two independent investigators judged whether there were no concerns, serious concerns, or very serious concerns. On the other hand, for the studies included in the meta-analysis, publication bias was also estimated using statistical methods based on the Egger’s Test and Funnel Plots for each clinical outcome.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: For studies reporting continuous variables as medians and ranges, data were converted to means and standard deviations (SD) following methods outlined by Hozo et al. [ 20 ]. Moreover, odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were reported for dichotomous variables. When outcomes of interest were not directly reported, data were extracted from Kaplan–Meier curves using WebPlotDigitizer. The choice between fixed-effect and random-effects models was determined separately for each outcome based on both statistical and clinical considerations. Due to variability in nutrient composition, dosage, timing of administration, and patient characteristics across studies, the random-effects model (using the REML method) was generally employed to account for between-study differences. However, for mortality, where heterogeneity was low (I² = 0.0%) and effect directions were consistent across studies, a fixed-effect model was applied to obtain a more precise pooled estimate. For other outcomes exhibiting substantial heterogeneity (I² >50%), the random-effects model was retained to better reflect the clinical diversity among studies. In response to editorial feedback, the entire meta-analysis was re-conducted using alternative statistical software ( STATA Software Version 17 ) under the supervision of a different analyst to validate the robustness of the results. The re-analysis confirmed consistency with the initial findings. To further explore and understand the sources of heterogeneity, a detailed leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was conducted. This approach allows for the assessment of how each individual study influences the pooled effect size and helps identify potential outliers contributing to overall heterogeneity. Although this method does not statistically eliminate heterogeneity, it enhances the interpretability and transparency of the pooled results. Such heterogeneity does not invalidate the synthesis; however, it requires cautious interpretation and transparent reporting. Due to the limited number of studies ( n = 5), conducting meta-regression or moderator analyses was not statistically justified, as these methods typically require approximately ten or more studies to ensure sufficient power and to avoid spurious results. Potential publication bias was evaluated by visual inspection of Funnel Plots and further assessed using Egger’s regression test. A p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All statistical analyses were performed using the “meta” and “metafor” packages in R (version 4.4.2, R Foundation for Statistical Computing).

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The results of our comprehensive systematic search are illustrated in Fig. 1 in detail. A total of 12 articles [ 1 , 12 , 21 – 30 ] met the predefined inclusion criteria and were included in the present study. The characteristics of these studies have been summarized in Table 1 . Among them, five studies [ 12 , 24 , 26 , 27 , 30 ] utilizing immunonutrition or immune-enhancing formulas for their interventions, compared with standard nutrition formulas, met the eligibility criteria for inclusion in our meta-analysis. Included studies exhibit moderate quality regarding bias risk, with no serious concern about the certainty of the body of evidence, as assessed by the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool and the GRADE approach, respectively. Table 2 summarizes the risk of bias ratings for each study. Funnel plots were generated for all main clinical outcomes to assess potential publication bias. Visual inspection suggested no pronounced asymmetry for hospital length of stay, ICU length of stay, and duration of mechanical ventilation, indicating low risk of small-study effects for these outcomes (Fig. 2 A–C). For mortality, the funnel plot showed slight asymmetry, and Egger’s test indicated a statistically significant bias ( p = 0.014). To account for this, a Trim-and-Fill analysis was performed, which suggested minimal impact on the pooled estimate (Fig. 2 D–E). These findings support the robustness of our meta-analysis results while highlighting that interpretation, particularly for mortality, should remain cautious due to potential publication bias. Although Egger’s test and funnel plots are statistically underpowered with fewer than ten studies, we presented them for completeness and transparency. The results should therefore be interpreted cautiously, as descriptive rather than confirmatory evidence of publication bias.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Overall, 12 studies were included in this systematic review; however, only 5 studies with 290 patients were included in the meta-analysis. We were unable to identify all predefined endpoints in each study for the meta-analysis. Ultimately, the outcomes for which the meta-analysis could be conducted included hospital LOS from 4 studies, ICU LOS from 5 studies, mortality rate from 4 studies, and duration of mechanical ventilation from 4 studies. Interventions mostly were immunonutrition formulas vs. standard formulas in patients who required enteral nutrition. Forest plots for the main clinical outcomes are presented in Fig. 3 . For mortality rate (Fig. 3 A), the pooled odds ratio slightly favored the intervention group, although the difference did not reach statistical significance (OR = 0.61, 95% CI 0.35, 1.04; p = 0.06). The heterogeneity among studies was negligible (I² = 0%), suggesting consistent findings across trials. For hospital length of stay (Fig. 3 B), the mean difference between groups was small and not significant (SMD − 0.67; 95% CI −1.46, 0.12; p = 0.09), with high heterogeneity (I² = 94.9%). Furthermore, ICU length of stay showed a modest but nonsignificant reduction in the intervention group (SMD − 0.40; 95% CI −0.90, 0.10; p = 0.12; I 2 = 88.0%; Fig. 3 C), For duration of mechanical ventilation (Fig. 3 D), the pooled estimate showed no significant difference (SMD 0.19; 95% CI −0.49, 0.88; p = 0.58; I 2 = 85.0%). Overall, these findings suggest that while the intervention may modestly reduce mortality, this effect was not statistically significant. Its impact on other clinical outcomes remains uncertain, partly due to substantial heterogeneity among the studies. Sensitivity analyses were conducted by sequentially omitting each study to assess the stability of the pooled estimates (Fig. 4 ). For mortality (Fig. 4 A), the overall effect size remained virtually unchanged (pooled OR = 0.61, 95% CI 0.36–1.04; I² = 0%), confirming the robustness of the mortality outcome. For hospital length of stay (Fig. 4 B), excluding any single study did not materially alter the overall estimate (SMD = − 0.48, 95% CI − 0.67 to − 0.30), although high heterogeneity persisted (I² ≈ 95%). Similarly, the pooled effect for ICU length of stay (Fig. 4 D) remained stable across iterations (SMD = − 0.18, 95% CI − 0.36 to − 0.01; I² = 86%), indicating that the observed reduction is consistent and not driven by any single study. For mechanical ventilation duration (Fig. 4 C), results were also robust, with only minor variation when individual studies were excluded (SMD = 0.32, 95% CI 0.12–0.53; I² = 85%). Overall, the sensitivity analysis supports the reliability of the main meta-analysis findings, demonstrating that no single study unduly influenced the pooled estimates. Such heterogeneity does not invalidate the synthesis; however, it requires cautious interpretation and transparent reporting.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The five studies included in our meta-analysis referred to their intervention as an immunonutrition formula compared to the standard formula, which consisted of a variety of nutrients (multicomponent) rather than a single nutrient. All the statistical findings on clinical outcomes mentioned above were derived from these multicomponent interventions. However, as our meta-analysis included only five studies, and each used a different immunonutrition formula with varying components, immunonutrition enriched with Omega-3 [ 12 ], immunonutrition enriched with Omega-3 + Glutamine + Arginine [ 24 ], with Alanine + Glutamine [ 26 ], with Omega-3 + Arginine [ 30 ], and immunonutrition enriched with some amino acids and other nutrients [ 27 ], there were no sufficiently similar groups of studies to allow meaningful subgroup analyses. In addition, meta-regression requires a larger number of studies (generally ≥ 10) to provide reliable results, which was not feasible in our case. On the other hand, for single-nutrient interventions, there was wide variation in components across studies. Moreover, either none of the clinical outcomes of interest were measured, or the inflammatory markers assessed were highly diverse, with each study measuring different types of markers. Consequently, we were unable to perform any statistical pooling of effects for these articles and therefore opted to provide a narrative description instead. This classification is important for interpreting outcomes, as the breadth and mechanisms of action differ substantially between these approaches and may help contextualize the conclusions.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Poblete RA et al. [ 12 ] conducted a prospective study involving 151 patients with severe TBI that demonstrated immunonutrition enriched with omega-3 fatty acids could improve the GCS score from an initial average of 4.8 to 5.9 by day 7. Although there was a minimal recovery in GCS, this difference was not statistically significant in the overall patient population, nor in the immunonutrition and control groups when analyzed separately.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: A RCT conducted by Noguchi H et al. [ 25 ] involving 110 patients with TBI (53 in the DHA group and 57 in the placebo group) demonstrated that daily supplementation with DHA at a dosage of 1800 mg/day significantly increased erythrocyte DHA levels in the DHA group after 12 weeks. However, there was no significant difference in health-related quality of life or the physical component summary (PCS) and mental component summary (MCS) scores between the two groups. They did not measure any inflammatory markers or other clinical outcomes in their study.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Nägeli M, et al. [ 23 ] conducted a RCT involving 12 patients with TBI, with 6 patients in each group. They administered 0.5 g/kg/day of glutamine for 5 days. The study found no secondary brain injuries, such as an increase in intracranial pressure (ICP) or a decrease in tissue oxygen pressure (ptiO2). Plasma levels of glutamine and alanine were elevated, while plasma urea and ammonia levels remained below significant thresholds. Similarly, Yang DL, et al. [ 21 ] conducted an RCT with 46 patients suffering from severe TBI, comparing the effects of glutamine and alanine peptides (2 mg/kg for 2 weeks) against standard nutritional support. The glutamine group experienced shorter ICU stay and exhibited lower mortality rates. A retrospective study conducted by Painter TJ et al. [ 24 ] analyzed 240 patients with TBI who received either an immune-enhancing nutrition (IEN) formula (containing glutamine, arginine, and omega-3) or a standard nutrition formula. The IEN group demonstrated improved prealbumin levels, reduced infection rates (specifically bacteremia), shorter stays in the ICU and hospital, and comparable mortality rates to those in the standard formula group.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The effects of nutritional supplementation on inflammatory markers have been investigated in several studies. Yang DL, et al. [ 21 ] observed increased serum protein levels and lymphocyte counts with glutamine supplementation. Similarly, Painter TJ et al. [ 24 ] indicated that prealbumin levels were higher in the immune-enhancing nutrition group compared to the control group receiving a standard nutrition formula. Furthermore, Rai VRH, et al. [ 1 ] conducted a RCT in which patients were randomly assigned to an immune nutrition group enriched with omega-3, glutamine, and arginine, or to a control group receiving standard enteral nutrition. In the immunonutrition group, there were increases in glutathione levels and total serum protein, along with a reduction in IL-6 levels. Chao K et al. [ 27 ] also found lower levels of IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, and TNF-α in the immune-enhancing nutrition group compared to the control group, while clinical outcomes remained unchanged between the two groups. Raikes AC, et al. [ 29 ] demonstrated an increase in NFL levels in the treatment group receiving omega-3 and concluded that it serves as a neuroprotective agent. A retrospective study by Poblete RA et al. [ 12 ] also reported on inflammatory markers, indicating that the levels of CRP were lower in the study group compared to the control group. However, this difference was not statistically significant, nor were there significant differences in clinical outcomes and mortality between the study group receiving immunonutrition enriched with omega-3 and the control group receiving a standard formula. The length of ICU and hospital stays in polytrauma patients showed significant differences between the two groups.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Some studies have reported the effects of interventions on clinical outcomes without mentioning inflammatory markers. The studies by Daia C, et al. [ 28 ] and Xiong W, et al. [ 26 ] are included in the present review. Both studies evaluated the effects of immunonutrition enriched with glutamine and found improvements in consciousness, restoration of functional independence, and reductions in adverse events, ICU length of stay, hospitalization duration, and ventilation time. However, Cotoia A, et al. [ 30 ] conducted a cohort study involving 31 traumatic brain injury (TBI) patients, comparing a specialized nutrition group enriched with omega-3 ( n = 16) and standard nutrition group ( n = 15). The study found no significant differences in the duration of mechanical ventilation, length of ICU stay, hospitalization, or 28-day mortality between the two groups.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Rangel-Castilla L, et al. [ 22 ] conducted a prospective study involving 51 patients with severe TBI. They observed that the administration of L-Arginine at a dosage of 300 mg/kg, given 12 h and 48 h post-injury, resulted in a significant improvement in CO2 reactivity; however, there was no change in dynamic pressure autoregulation.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: This systematic review and meta-analysis synthesized current evidence regarding the impact of immunonutrition and its key components on inflammatory markers and clinical outcomes in patients with TBI. Although data remain limited, particularly for RCTs using complete immunonutrition formulas, our findings suggest potential but inconclusive benefits. Immunonutrition appeared to reduce mortality, and the length of hospital and ICU stays compared with standard nutrition; however, these effects did not reach statistical significance. The limited number of eligible trials and the small, pooled sample size reduce the power to detect clinically meaningful differences. Therefore, while the overall direction of the effect is favorable, these results should be interpreted as exploratory rather than confirmatory evidence. Marked heterogeneity among included studies further limits the strength of inference. Differences in timing and route of administration, variations in nutrient composition (e.g., omega-3 fatty acids, arginine, glutamine, or multicomponent formulas), baseline injury severity, and study design likely contributed to variability across outcomes. Such inconsistencies highlight the lack of standardized nutritional protocols in TBI research and underscore the urgent need for rigorously designed clinical trials using harmonized interventions. Furthermore, of the 12 included studies, only 5 were eligible for inclusion in the meta-analysis, which may reduce statistical power and increase the risk of a type II error. Despite these limitations, a random-effects model and sensitivity analyses confirmed the robustness of the direction of effect, supporting a cautiously optimistic interpretation that immunonutrition may contribute to improved recovery in TBI when appropriately tailored.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Immunonutrition, or immune-enhancing nutrition, typically includes arginine, glutamine, omega-3 PUFAs, and nucleotides [ 31 ]. Various studies have used different combinations, ranging from two to all four, across multiple studies in diverse clinical settings [ 1 , 12 , 24 , 27 , 28 ]. Furthermore, most studies have reported single interventions with each of the immunonutrition components, particularly omega-3 PUFAs, administered through various routes, including oral, enteral, and intravenous, with differing dosages as supplements [ 25 , 29 , 32 , 33 ]. Our review retrieved five studies on multi-component formulas, three on glutamine, two on arginine, and four on fish oil interventions in hospitalized TBI patients.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: ESPEN guidelines recommend immunonutrition perioperatively for improving immune markers and reducing complications, particularly infections [ 34 – 38 ]. However, its benefits and safety in critically ill ICU patients remain uncertain. High-arginine formulas have been shown to reduce infections in critically ill surgical patients but may be associated with increased mortality in critically ill medical patients, according to some high-quality studies [ 39 , 40 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Overall, modulation of inflammatory biomarkers (reductions in IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8, IL-10; increases in glutathione and total protein) corresponded with trends toward improved clinical outcomes, including shorter ICU/hospital stays, lower infection rates, and better functional recovery [ 1 , 12 , 21 , 24 , 27 ]. Although statistical significance was inconsistent due to small sample sizes and heterogeneity, the link between immune modulation and favorable outcomes appears biologically plausible.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Arginine modulates immunity via nitric oxide synthesis [ 22 , 30 , 39 – 41 ]. While perioperative studies indicate benefits, concerns about hemodynamic instability in critically ill TBI patients persist [ 41 ]. Evidence suggests 300 mg/kg arginine post-TBI did not significantly alter MAP, ICP, or brain oxygenation while improving CO₂ reactivity [ 22 ]. Arginine combined with omega-3 had no major effect on lung microbiota [ 30 ]. Its role is promising but requires further study, with current guidelines recommending supplementation mainly for burn or multi-trauma patients [ 42 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Glutamine, like arginine, is suggested for severe burns and multiple traumas [ 42 ]. Despite limited survival benefits even in burned adults [ 43 ], it serves as a precursor for energy and glutathione synthesis [ 23 , 44 , 45 ]. Critically ill TBI patients often exhibit hypoglutaminemia, linked to poor prognosis [ 23 , 45 ]. Three studies showed supplementation can reverse hypoglutaminemia, reduce infections, and shorten ICU/hospital stays, though 28-day survival was unaffected [ 21 , 23 , 26 ]. High-quality RCTs are needed to confirm efficacy.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Omega-3 fatty acids, particularly EPA and DHA, have neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and membrane-stabilizing effects, limit excitotoxicity, and promote neurogenesis in TBI patients [ 33 , 46 – 49 ]. While much of the literature is based on animal and cell studies, several human studies, including two omega-3-specific and five multi-component immunonutrition studies enriched with omega-3, were included in this review (Table 3 ).

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: These interventions were linked to improved functional connectivity, better long-term quality of life, and, in some subgroups, shorter ICU and hospital stays [ 12 , 25 , 29 ]. Effects on inflammatory markers and infection rates were variable [ 12 , 29 ]. TBI induces profound inflammation, with resting energy expenditure increasing up to 140%, microglial activation, innate immune hyperactivity, and myelin damage promoting chronic inflammation [ 33 , 41 ]. Unlike many anti-inflammatory agents, EPA and DHA cross the blood–brain barrier, achieving similar concentrations in serum and brain tissue [ 33 , 49 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: In individual studies, Raikes et al. [ 29 ] found no protection against axonal damage on neuroimaging but observed benefits for functional connectivity, indicating the need for larger trials. Another trial highlighted omega-3 as a safe, low-cost intervention with potential benefits on brain structure, membrane integrity, metabolism, and inflammation [ 33 ]. Overall, the evidence is promising but inconsistent, reinforcing the need for high-quality RCTs in TBI.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: The effects and certainty of the evidence are summarized in Table 3 , and both multi-component and single-nutrient perspectives are considered below.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Five studies examined multi-component formulas combining omega-3, arginine, glutamine, and nucleotides, with omega-3 predominant [ 1 , 12 , 24 , 27 , 28 ]. Glutamine reversed hypoglutaminemia and reduced infections [ 21 , 23 , 26 ], while arginine was well tolerated without major hemodynamic effects [ 22 , 30 ]. These interventions increased interferon-γ, glutathione, total protein, and prealbumin, decreased TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-10, and showed trends toward shorter ICU/hospital stays, though mortality reduction was not statistically significant.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: improved functional connectivity and quality of life, with mixed effects on inflammation and infection [ 25 , 29 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: corrected hypoglutaminemia, enhanced antioxidant status, reduced ICU/hospital stay and ventilator days, but minimal survival impact [ 21 , 23 , 26 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: immune modulation via nitric oxide pathways, safe dosing, no major hemodynamic effects, limited impact on lung microbiota when combined with omega-3 [ 22 , 30 ].

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Severe TBI induces hypermetabolism, amino/fatty acid depletion, and a pro-inflammatory yet immunosuppressed state, increasing infection risk. Early immunonutrition (5–7 days) combining omega-3, arginine, glutamine, and antioxidants reduces mortality, infections, and functional decline. Formula composition is critical to deliver adequate EPA/DHA for neuroprotection. Strategies include omega-3 (EPA + DHA ≥ 1.2 g/L) in irrigation fluids, postoperative L-arginine supplementation (300 mg/kg/day) to enhance CO2 reactivity, and glutamine supplementation (0.5 g/kg/day) to safely improve plasma and cerebral levels, infections, and outcomes. Multi-component formulas support functional recovery in chronic consciousness disorders and reduce bacteremia, highlighting the importance of patient selection and formula optimization.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Several limitations of the present evidence should be acknowledged. First, of the 12 included studies, only 5 were eligible for inclusion in the meta-analysis, which may reduce statistical power and increase the risk of a Type II error. To mitigate this issue, we applied a random-effects model and conducted leave-one-out sensitivity analyses. Both approaches confirmed the robustness and consistency of the effect direction across studies. However, this limitation primarily reflects the current scarcity of eligible trials in this specific field (severe TBI receiving immunonutrition). Furthermore, there is a scarcity of adequately powered RCTs in this field. Most included RCTs had sample sizes below 50 participants. For example, Yang et al. ( n = 46) [ 21 ], Rai et al. ( n = 36) [ 1 ], and Nägeli et al. ( n = 12) [ 23 ], which limits statistical power, reduces precision of pooled effect estimates, and increases the risk of type II error, constraining generalizability to the broader severe TBI population. Larger, multicenter RCTs such as Noguchi et al. [ 25 ] remain exceptions and occasionally focus on subgroups not fully representative of critically ill patients.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Second, while high-quality observational data [ 12 , 22 , 24 , 28 ] expanded the evidence base, inherent risks of residual confounding, selection bias, and differences in co-interventions remain, which could influence outcomes independently of true biological effects. For instance, certain formulas may have been preferentially administered to postoperative or polytrauma patients, potentially biasing results in favor of immunonutrition.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Third, reporting of nutrient composition, concentration, and dosing was inconsistent or incomplete. Several studies named commercial products (e.g., Vital High Protein™, Nomune) without specifying contents of omega-3 fatty acids, glutamine, arginine, or adjunct compounds [ 12 , 29 ]. Doses varied widely, from DHA + EPA totals of 2.442 g/day DHA + 1.020 g/day EPA [ 27 ] to blended formulas delivering < 2 g total omega-3/day, and glutamine from 0.3 g/kg/day [ 26 ] to 0.5 g/kg/day [ 23 ]. Lack of standardized composition precluded dose–response analyses or identification of optimal therapeutic thresholds.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Fourth, intervention timing, route, and duration were heterogeneous. Rai et al. [ 1 ] initiated feeding within 24–48 h of admission, whereas Poblete et al. [ 12 ] did not clearly specify timing. Duration ranged from single-day infusions [ 23 ] to 7-day ICU regimens [ 26 ] and months-long supplementation [ 25 ], affecting both biological plausibility and comparability. These and previous limitations do not diminish the importance of our analysis; rather, they highlight that studies in this area are limited and fragmented. There is a clear need for future repeated trials with fully matched and standardized specifications regarding immunonutrition interventions.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Fifth, clinical and methodological heterogeneity was substantial, as reflected in high I² values for most outcomes except mortality. Variability arose from patient case mix (isolated TBI vs. polytrauma; post-craniotomy vs. conservative management), baseline severity (GCS/AIS), nutritional risk, and co-interventions (surgery, ICP management). Outcome definitions and measurement schedules also varied, reflecting differences in study design, patient characteristics, intervention timing, nutrient composition, and administration routes.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Sixth, meta-regression to explore potential effect modifiers was not feasible due to fewer than 10 studies per covariate, limiting assessment of moderators such as surgical status, baseline nutritional risk, timing of feeding, or injury mechanism. Consequently, uncertainty remains regarding whether specific subgroups, e.g., stable post-craniotomy patients with severe isolated TBI, may benefit more from immunonutrition.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Seventh, although our goal was to analyze inflammatory factors to calculate a pooled effect size, the lack of eligible studies and their heterogeneity made a meta-analysis impractical. Consequently, we relied on a narrative synthesis. This underscores the need for future research to accurately assess the impact of immunonutrition on inflammatory markers (e.g., IL-6, CRP) in patients with severe TBI.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Eighth, another limitation of the present meta-analysis is that tests for publication bias (such as Egger’s test and funnel plots) were conducted despite the small number of included studies (< 10). Although these analyses are statistically underpowered in such cases, they were presented for transparency and should be interpreted descriptively rather than conclusively. This approach aligns with current Cochrane recommendations, which emphasize that reporting funnel plots in small meta-analyses is acceptable when accompanied by a clear and cautious interpretation of their limited reliability.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Finally, despite the use of random-effects modeling with REML estimation and leave-one-out sensitivity analyses, residual uncertainty persists due to small RCT sizes, observational bias, incomplete dosing/formula reporting, intervention variability, and outcome heterogeneity. Collectively, these factors limit the strength and generalizability of conclusions. Addressing these gaps will require large, multicenter, phase III RCTs with transparent nutrient composition, stratified randomization, standardized outcomes, and harmonized feeding protocols to enable robust pooling and subgroup analyses in future meta-analyses.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Advancing immunonutrition in neurosurgical ICU patients requires well-powered, multicenter, randomized phase III trials with standardized protocols. Future studies should stratify participants by baseline GCS, surgical status, injury mechanism, and nutritional risk, clearly defining formula composition, dosing, delivery route, and timing (ideally within 48 h post-injury). Primary endpoints should include validated functional recovery, neurocognitive performance, quality of life, and healthcare utilization, beyond survival. Mechanistic biomarkers (cerebral microdialysate, multimodal neuromonitoring, MRI neuroinflammation, serum NFL, oxidative stress markers) can clarify biological pathways and identify responders. Economic analyses will help assess cost-effectiveness. Subgroup analyses should evaluate differences between polytrauma versus isolated TBI, surgical populations, and patients receiving targeted amino acids at studied doses.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated immunonutrition, primarily omega-3 fatty acids, glutamine, and arginine, showing consistent biomarker improvements and favorable physiological effects, though statistically significant impacts on hard clinical endpoints were not observed. The biological plausibility, reproducibility of biomarker changes, and safety in ICU settings support further research. Standardized, early, and adequately dosed interventions are most likely to translate biochemical modulation into meaningful neurocritical care outcomes.

metadata.title: Immunonutrition for modifying inflammatory markers and improving clinical outcomes following traumatic brain injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

text: Shock is a life-threatening condition marked by impaired oxygen delivery, heightened oxygen demand, or the body’s inability to utilize oxygen effectively—ultimately leading to cellular and tissue hypoxia [ 1 ]. Clinically, it often presents as hypotension and arises from a wide range of underlying causes, all of which carry high mortality if not promptly recognized and treated. Shock is broadly categorized into four types: distributive, hypovolemic, cardiogenic, and obstructive. A notable subtype, vasoplegic shock, falls under distributive shock and is defined by an abnormally low systemic vascular resistance (SVR) despite normal or even elevated cardiac output. This mismatch results in dangerously low blood pressure and poor tissue perfusion. Vasoplegic shock commonly occurs in early sepsis, after cardiopulmonary bypass, during severe anaphylaxis, or in the aftermath of trauma resuscitation. Uniquely challenging, it often proves refractory to standard fluid resuscitation and conventional adrenergic vasopressors, making it a critical therapeutic dilemma.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: We present the use of a novel vasoactive agent, angiotensin II (AT-II), to treat intraoperative vasoplegic shock during bilateral nephrectomy in an infant. Options to treat vasoplegic shock are discussed, previous reports of the use of AT-II in infants and children reviewed, and its role in the treatment of vasoplegic shock presented.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Review of this case and presentation followed the guidelines of the Institutional Review Board of Nationwide Children’s Hospital (Columbus, OH). Written, informed consent was obtained for the use of de-identified information for publication.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: The patient was a 5-week-old male infant with stage 5 chronic kidney disease secondary to autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD), who presented for bilateral nephrectomy and placement of a peritoneal dialysis catheter.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: The patient was born at 33 weeks weighing 2,140 g by vaginal delivery to a 20-year-old female. Pregnancy was complicated by late prenatal care starting at 30 weeks. At that time, an ultrasound showed anhydramnios and magnetic resonance imaging revealed bilaterally enlarged kidneys concerning for ARPKD. The patient was admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) after birth where ARPKD was confirmed by ultrasound. The patient was also found to have a large right pneumothorax, small pericardial effusion, and pulmonary hypoplasia on chest radiograph. Respiratory insufficiency was initially treated with continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) followed by endotracheal intubation and surfactant administration. The pneumothorax was treated by needle thoracostomy shortly after birth and aquapheresis was started for management of his volume status. Surgery occurred at the age of 5 weeks, with a patient weight of 3 kg. Immediately prior to surgery, the patient was receiving erythropoietin to stimulate red blood cell production, nicardipine (2 - 3 µg/kg/min) to maintain the mean arterial pressure (MAP) at 45 - 60 mm Hg, fentanyl (1.5 µg/kg/h) to provide sedation during mechanical ventilation, and bivalirudin to provide anticoagulation during aquapheresis. The preoperative physical examination was notable for significantly enlarged, distended, and palpable kidneys bilaterally, along with trace non-pitting peripheral edema. The patient was afebrile with a pulse of 147 beats/min, with a ventilator rate of 40 breaths/min, pressure support 12 cm H 2 O, and oxygen saturation of 97% with an inspired oxygen concentration of 35%. The trachea had been previously intubated with a 3.0 mm endotracheal tube (ETT). Cardiovascular exam was significant for a systolic murmur with a regular rate and rhythm with normal peripheral pulses and capillary refill of 2 s. The preoperative complete blood count including hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelet count was within normal range. Laboratory evaluation revealed sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, and glucose of 100 mg/dL. Although the preoperative blood urea nitrogen and creatinine were elevated at 52 and 1.28 mg/dL, respectively, the values were stable over the past week. The prothrombin time was elevated, but stable at 15.5 s with an international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.2; activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) was elevated at 55 with a normal fibrinogen.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: The patient was kept nil per os for 8 h prior to surgery and transported to the operating room, where standard American Society of Anesthesiologists’ monitors were applied. Anesthesia was maintained with sevoflurane (expired concentration 0.5-1.5%), dexmedetomidine (1.5 µg/kg/h), and fentanyl (2 - 3 µg/kg/h). Neuromuscular blockade was provided by intermittent doses of rocuronium. MAP was maintained at 45 - 60 mm Hg with nicardipine (1 - 2 µg/kg/min). Additional intraoperative analgesia was provided by two bolus doses of morphine (0.1 mg/kg/dose). Dextrose 20% in 0.9% sodium chloride was infused at 10 mL/h via a pre-existing central venous catheter. Prophylaxis against surgical site infections included cefazolin (50 mg/kg) every 3 h. Intraoperatively, during the bilateral nephrectomy, the nicardipine infusion was discontinued as the MAP was less than 40 mm Hg. A further decrease in the MAP failed to respond to fluid administration (20 - 30 mL/kg of 5% albumin), and vasopressor support was initiated with epinephrine at 0.02 µg/kg/min when the MAP persisted at 30 mm Hg. No response was noted when the epinephrine was increased to 0.05 µg/kg/min. Approximately 15 min after the start of the epinephrine infusion, presuming that the low MAP was related to vasoplegic shock, an AT-II infusion was started at 5 ng/kg/min and then increased over the next 15 - 20 min to 10 ng/kg/min, resulting in an MAP of 50 - 60 mm Hg. The MAP was sustained throughout the remainder of the procedure at 50 - 60 mm Hg. Postoperatively, AT-II was continued at 5 - 10 ng/kg/min to maintain the MAP at 50 - 70 mm Hg as the epinephrine infusion was weaned and then discontinued. On postoperative day 3, the AT-II infusion was slowly weaned in increments of 1 ng/kg/min over 18 - 24 h and then discontinued. On postoperative day 2, patient was given hydrocortisone to support MAPs.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Postoperatively, the patient was continued on aquapheresis to maintain his volume status and has subsequently been transitioned to peritoneal dialysis and was stable on his ventilator settings. His hemodynamic and respiratory status were stable during the postoperative course. Given the presence of multiple comorbid conditions including chronic respiratory failure and the ongoing need for PD, he remains hospitalized in the NICU to address these issues.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Vasoplegia, marked by a dangerously low SVR, can arise from a range of critical conditions—including sepsis, anaphylaxis, and the inflammatory aftermath of cardiopulmonary bypass. Its pathophysiology is multifaceted, driven by a complex interplay of intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms that disrupt normal vascular tone. On the intrinsic side, potent vasodilators such as nitric oxide (NO), endothelin-1, and prostanoids are key contributors [ 2 - 5 ]. Meanwhile, extrinsic influences—particularly the vascular system’s diminished responsiveness to endogenous vasoconstrictors like catecholamines, vasopressin, and glucocorticoids—further amplify and prolong the vasoplegic state. This intricate balance between molecular signaling and vascular reactivity makes vasoplegia a particularly challenging condition to manage. The primary goals in the treatment of vasoplegic shock are twofold: identifying and addressing the underlying cause, and providing hemodynamic support to maintain adequate oxygen delivery and tissue perfusion. Management typically involves targeted therapy based on the etiology, along with interventions aimed at restoring MAP. These may include careful fluid resuscitation and the use of vasoactive agents to counteract the profound systemic vasodilation characteristic of the low SVR state.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Successful management of vasoplegic shock requires a balance of maintaining adequate SVR to ensure organ perfusion while mitigating adverse effects associated with high-dose vasopressor therapy [ 2 ]. Traditional pharmacological approaches to vasoplegic shock primarily involve α-adrenergic agonists such as phenylephrine or norepinephrine, dopamine, and non-adrenergic vasoactive agents such as vasopressin [ 3 , 4 ]. These agents may have a narrow therapeutic index, with escalating doses leading to dose-limiting toxicities including myocardial ischemia, arrhythmias, and peripheral vasoconstriction that can compromise organ perfusion. Adjunctive therapies have included the administration of corticosteroids or methylene blue, which inhibits guanylate cyclase, an important enzyme in the nitric oxide signaling pathway [ 5 , 6 ]. Corticosteroids increase the responsive of the adrenergic receptors of the vasculature, increasing cytosolic calcium availability, and increasing vascular tone. In adult ICU practice, hydrocortisone has been used alone or as part of hydrocortisone, ascorbic acid and thiamine (HAT) therapy that includes hydrocortisone, ascorbic acid, and thiamine. Dopamine has been used as a naturally occurring catecholamine and precursor to norepinephrine to increase systemic arterial pressure and stimulate the myocardium.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Given the limitations and potential toxicities of conventional therapies discussed above, there has been growing interest in alternative agents with novel mechanisms of action—most notably, AT-II, a naturally occurring hormone with potent vasoconstrictive properties and a central role in cardiovascular homeostasis. AT-II is an octapeptide and a key component of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), a hormonal system involved in regulating blood pressure, fluid, and electrolyte balance [ 7 ]. AT-II exerts potent vasoconstrictor effects primarily through activation of AT-II type 1 receptors located on vascular smooth muscle cells. Binding to these receptors triggers a cascade of intracellular events, including increased cytosolic calcium, leading to smooth muscle contraction and subsequent vasoconstriction. Beyond its direct vasoconstrictive properties, AT-II also promotes aldosterone release, sodium reabsorption, and can modulate activity of the sympathetic nervous system.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: Preliminary clinical experience with exogenous AT-II has shown promise in adults with refractory vasoplegic shock. The Angiotensin II for the Treatment of High-Output Shock (ATHOS-3) trial, an international, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 344 adult patients, demonstrated improved MAP in adults with vasodilatory shock refractory to conventional vasopressor therapy, defined as shock being treated with more than 0.2 µg/kg/min of norepinephrine, or the equivalent dose of another vasopressor [ 8 ]. Response to angiotensin was defined as an increase in the MAP to ≥ 75 mm Hg or an increase in MAP from baseline ≥ 10 mm Hg without an increase in the dose of other vasopressor agent. Significantly more patients in the AT-II arm than in the placebo arm achieved the primary end point. Additionally, there was a greater increase in MAP in the AT-II group than placebo. The trial highlighted AT-II as a viable therapeutic option for patients with distributive shock unresponsive to adrenergic agents and vasopressin.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: While well-established in adult critical care, the published literature on AT-II use in pediatric patients, particularly infants, has been limited [ 9 - 12 ]. Bailey et al outlined the use of AT-II in two pediatric patients (2 and 8 years of age) who required high-dose vasopressor therapy in the setting of vasoplegic shock [ 10 ]. The first patient was in vasodilatory septic shock as a result of pneumonia and was receiving norepinephrine 0.05 µg/kg/min and dopamine 15 µg/kg/min prior to the initiation of AT-II at 1.25 ng/kg/min. Three hours after starting AT-II, all other traditional vasopressor therapy was successfully discontinued. The second patient in this case series was admitted with septic shock in the setting of meningitis and encephalitis. There was no response to multiple vasoactive infusions (epinephrine 0.1 µg/kg/min, norepinephrine 0.2 µg/kg/min, and vasopressin 2 mU/kg/min). AT-II was started at 1.25 ng/kg/min and eventually increased to 39.1 ng/kg/min. Within 90 min of initiation, the norepinephrine and vasopressin infusions were weaned and discontinued. Due to a progressive central nervous system (CNS) insult, the patient developed refractory cerebral edema, increased intracranial pressure (ICP), and expired. Additional anecdotal experience was reported by Razdan et al in their use of AT-II to treat vasodilatory shock on day of life 5 in a preterm infant with bilateral renal agenesis [ 11 ]. Vasodilatory shock was unresponsive to the administration of dopamine, vasopressin, epinephrine, and hydrocortisone. AT-II was started at 1.25 ng/kg/min and eventually titrated to up 40 ng/kg/min over 4 days. Within the first day of AT-II therapy, the patient’s blood pressure had stabilized and other vasoactive agents were discontinued.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: In the largest cohort to date in infants and children, Tezel et al reported their experience using AT-II in a cohort of 23 children (median age 10.4 years) from a single-center, retrospective case series [ 12 ]. The majority of patients presented with vasodilatory shock secondary to sepsis and severe acute kidney injury and required high-dose vasopressor therapy with a median norepinephrine equivalent dose (NED) of 0.65 µg/kg/min. AT-II was started at a median dose of 10 ng/kg/min with a median infusion duration of 27 h. At each assessment point, there was an improvement in the NED and MAP. Although overall survival in the cohort was limited to 30%, the authors observed that survivors received AT-II therapy significantly earlier than non-survivors—by nearly 3 days (91.5 vs. 161 h)—highlighting the potential importance of timely intervention. The authors concluded that AT-II decreased NED and improved MAP in critically ill children with catecholamine-resistant vasodilatory shock (CRVS). They further recommended further prospective work to examine optimal timing of AT-II therapy and patient selection.

metadata.title: Angiotensin II to Treat Intraoperative Vasoplegic Shock in an Infant.

text: In summary, our case contributes to the growing body of anecdotal evidence supporting the potential utility of AT-II in pediatric patients with distributive or vasoplegic shock refractory to conventional adrenergic therapy. While data in this population remain limited, these observations—when considered alongside more robust findings from adult studies—suggest a role for AT-II, particularly in cases involving escalating vasoactive support without adequate improvement in MAP.

text: Sepsis is a complex failure in organ function caused by a disordered systemic response of the host against infection [ 1 ]. Manifestations of sepsis depend on various factors, including the complex interaction between the host response and the infected organism causing these conditions [ 2 ]. Various studies show that the imbalance between inflammatory and anti-inflammatory responses leads to irreversible tissue-damaging disorders and multi-organ failure in sepsis [ 3 ]. An adjunctive therapy to limit the inflammatory response, reduce the damage caused by free radicals, and improve metabolic disorders can lead to the fight against sepsis [ 4 ].

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: Administration of vitamin C has demonstrated benefits in severe sepsis and septic shock, including reductions in Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) scores, shock treatment duration, and inflammatory biomarkers [ 5 , 6 ]. As an immune-modulating factor and cofactor for iron-containing enzymes, it requires external supplementation in mammals due to the absence of gluconolactone oxidase [ 7 – 9 ].

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: In various studies, statin, a cholesterol-lowering medicine, has been discussed in terms of their pleiotropic benefits for sepsis [ 10 ]. Studies have shown that the use of high-dose statin is associated with lower mortality in sepsis compared to the use of a low-dose statin regime [ 6 ]. It is also known that statin has pleiotropic effects independent of cholesterol, such as improving endothelial function, reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, and reducing thrombogenicity, which helps in the treatment of sepsis, which is a systemic inflammatory syndrome caused by infection [ 10 , 11 ]. Therefore, statin is considered new therapeutic agents that may help to treat and prevent sepsis by targeting the inflammatory and immunomodulatory cascades involved in the development of sepsis [ 12 , 13 ].

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: Based on our best knowledge, the effects of the two supportive treatments (statin and vitamin C) have been investigated separately, but so far no research has investigated the combined effect of the treatments in patients with septic shock and severe septic shock. The complementary action mechanisms of vitamin C and statin, particularly relevant in pulmonary septic shock encouraged us to focus on this title. Vitamin C provides antioxidant properties that reduce oxidative stress and inflammation [ 14 , 15 ], while atorvastatin offers pleiotropic effects including improved endothelial function and reduced thrombogenicity [ 16 , 17 ]. For this reasons, and considering the extensive complications, the high mortality rate of this disorder, and the need to use cheap and available supportive treatments [ 18 , 19 ], the present study was conducted to investigate the combined effect of these two factors on sepsis and severe sepsis.

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: The study as a single-center and double-blind controlled clinical trial was conducted at Labafi Nejad Hospital, Tehran, Iran, in the Respiratory ICU, a specialized unit for critically ill patients with respiratory issues. After obtaining informed consent, the patients admitted to a referral hospital with sepsis were assigned to control and intervention groups. Inclusion criteria were age ≥ 18 years, definite diagnosis of pulmonary septic shock according to Sepsis-3 criteria, hospitalization in respiratory ICU, multi-organ involvement (failure of at least two organs or SOFA score ≥ 2 for two or more organ systems), and obtaining informed consent from patient or legal representative. And exclusion criteria were:

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: Known allergy or hypersensitivity to vitamin C or atorvastatin,

metadata.title: The combined effect of statin and vitamin C in patients with pulmonary septic shock: a single-center, double-blind clinical trial.

text: Serious underlying diseases including end-stage malignancy with life expectancy < 3 months, end-stage liver disease (Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis), end-stage renal disease requiring chronic dialysis, severe heart failure (NYHA Class IV) or cardiogenic shock, severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with baseline oxygen dependency, active bleeding or coagulopathy (INR > 2.0 not due to anticoagulation), Immunocompromised state (active chemotherapy, organ transplant recipients, HIV with CD4 < 200).
